_notes: LNG terminals and helium production facilities in the Gulf. All Qatar LNG and associated helium must transit Hormuz — there is no bypass.

lng_terminals.name: Ras Laffan LNG Complex
lng_terminals.damage_notes: Iranian missile strike March 2026 damaged infrastructure. QatarEnergy CEO stated 17% capacity reduction for up to 5 years.
lng_terminals.notes: World's largest LNG export complex. Feeds Qatar's entire LNG trade plus ~32% of global helium supply as byproduct of natural gas processing.

lng_terminals.name: Das Island LNG (ADGAS)
lng_terminals.notes: UAE LNG. Can use Fujairah pipeline bypass for condensate; LNG tankers still technically need Hormuz but UAE is on Gulf of Oman side approach.

lng_terminals.name: Oman LNG (Qalhat / Sur)
lng_terminals.notes: Oman is outside the Persian Gulf — on the Gulf of Oman coast. Oman LNG does NOT require Hormuz transit. Critical alternative supplier.

helium_sources.name: Qatar / Ras Laffan Helium Plants
helium_sources.notes: Helium extracted as byproduct of LNG processing. Goes offline when LNG production stops. Single largest source — 32% of global supply cut when Ras Laffan impacted.

helium_sources.name: US Federal Helium Reserve (Cliffside, Texas)
helium_sources.notes: Largest single helium reserve in world. Located in Hugoton gas field, Texas/Kansas/Oklahoma. US is global swing producer but has limited surge capacity. Reserve is depleting — fields in decline.

helium_sources.name: Russia (Amur / Siberia)
helium_sources.notes: Sanctioned since 2022; effectively unavailable to Western markets. Amur Gas Processing Plant came online 2021.

helium_sources.name: Australia (Darwin LNG / emerging)
helium_sources.notes: Emerging producer. Insufficient to replace Qatar volumes.

helium_mechanics.description: Helium evaporates from ISO containers even when sealed. Once stranded at a port, containers empty in approximately 35-48 days. Supply destruction is permanent — helium returns to atmosphere at concentrations too diffuse to recapture economically.

key_consumers.notes: Wafer cooling in lithography; leak detection in sub-5nm fabs. Lines stop, not slow, when helium runs out.
Superconducting magnet cooling to near absolute zero. No substitute gas. Hospitals ration scans during shortages.
Rocket fuel system purging, tank pressurization, leak testing. Cannot substitute.
Inert atmosphere for fiber drawing. Affects global telecommunications infrastructure manufacturing.
Cryogenic cooling for quantum computing research; particle physics experiments.

south_korea_dependency.notes: South Korea sources ~65% of industrial helium from Qatar. Samsung and SK Hynix are the world's two largest memory chip manufacturers.
